ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a croissant sandwich with ham, scrambled egg, and hashbrown. The broader 'sandwich food product' term (FOODON_00002473) is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sandwich — composite meat and egg filled pastry-based product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for croissant-based breakfast sandwiches with hashbrown. Code A0CKP (sandwich) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a filled pastry-based composite meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to this composite breakfast sandwich product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g, comprising one filled croissant (approximately 130–140 g) and one hashbrown patty (approximately 60–70 g), as commonly served at Singapore café and fast-food outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Ham and Scrambled Egg Croissant with Hashbrown — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Ham and Scrambled Egg Croissant with Hashbrown' (cr_id F08020038). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (croissant + fried egg + ham + hashbrown) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; croissant GI reference and fried potato GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite meal. GI estimated as a weighted average of the two primary carbohydrate-contributing components: (1) croissant GI ≈ 67 (University of Sydney GI Database; white-flour croissant, reference glucose = 100), contributing approximately 60% of available carbohydrate; (2) deep-fried hashbrown / fried potato GI ≈ 80 (University of Sydney GI Database; french fries / fried potato analogue), contributing approximately 40% of available carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI = (0.60 × 67) + (0.40 × 80) = 40.2 + 32.0 = 72.2, rounded to 72. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (72 × 16.06 × 2.00) / 100 = 23.1 g. Classified as high GI (≥70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 424 mg/100 g with negligible potassium (17 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.72 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold with virtually no potassium offset; individuals with hypertension should avoid this meal or limit consumption to occasional small portions, and must not add condiments such as ketchup or soy sauce that further increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High composite GI (~72), low dietary fibre (1.72 g/100 g), deep-fried hashbrown, and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of a refined-flour croissant and deep-fried hashbrown produces a high glycaemic load (~23 per serving) with minimal fibre buffering; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this meal and substitute with a wholemeal bread sandwich and baked potato to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (24.54 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~7.2 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and high sodium (424 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated and trans fat content from the croissant pastry and deep-fried hashbrown, combined with elevated sodium from processed ham, makes this meal unsuitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; regular consumption should be avoided in favour of lower-fat, lower-sodium breakfast alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation (AGE formation), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and arachidonic acid from processed pork ham
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying of the hashbrown triggers advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation, a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis; the estimated trans fat from croissant pastry is strongly pro-inflammatory, and individuals with RA should avoid this meal entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham and Scrambled Egg Croissant with Hashbrown is a Western-style breakfast set meal commonly served at Singapore cafés, fast-food outlets, and school canteens. It consists of a buttery, flaky croissant made from laminated white-flour pastry, filled with sliced picnic ham (processed pork) and pan-fried scrambled egg, served alongside a deep-fried hashbrown patty made from shredded potato. The croissant provides a rich, buttery flavour and soft-yet-flaky texture; the scrambled egg adds creaminess; the ham contributes a salty, savoury note; and the hashbrown delivers a crispy, golden exterior with a soft potato interior. Nutritionally, the meal is energy-dense at 341 kcal/100 g, with high total fat (24.54 g/100 g) dominated by saturated fat from the croissant pastry and frying oil, and elevated sodium (424 mg/100 g) from the processed ham and hashbrown. Dietary fibre is low at 1.72 g/100 g and potassium is negligible at 17 mg/100 g. The composite glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high GI), driven by the refined-flour croissant and deep-fried potato. Clinically, this meal is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium, negligible potassium), type 2 diabetes (high GI, low fibre, trans fat), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat, sodium), or rheumatoid arthritis (deep-fried AGEs, trans fat). It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions, with condiments avoided.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020038), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database approved sources, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy: croissant (~5.5 g sat fat/100 g per USDA SR Legacy #18023), scrambled egg (~3.3 g/100 g per USDA #01132), picnic ham (~2.5 g/100 g per USDA #10147), and deep-fried hashbrown (~2.0 g/100 g per USDA #11355), weighted by approximate component proportions (croissant 40%, egg 20%, ham 15%, hashbrown 25%). HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this composite item. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from croissant pastry lamination (partially hydrogenated fat contribution) and deep-frying oil; this is consistent with the input record's health_suitability notes. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, and all vitamin/mineral fields beyond those reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and micronutrient data for this composite Singapore fast-food item are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. 4. GI of 72 is a weighted analogous estimate (croissant GI 67 × 0.60 + fried potato GI 80 × 0.40) from University of Sydney GI Database approved references; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite meal exists in the published literature. 5. Potassium (17 mg/100 g) and calcium (31 mg/100 g) are as reported by HPB Foodo; potassium is unusually low, consistent with the dominance of refined pastry and processed meat in the composition. 6. Halal status is marked false as picnic ham is pork-derived; this meal is not suitable for halal dietary requirements.
